content.problemStatement:
Chef had an array AAA of length NNN such that 1≤Ai≤N1 \le A_i \le N1≤Ai​≤N for all 1≤i≤N1 \le i \le N1≤i≤N. Chef constructed another **binary** array BBB of length NNN in the following manner:


Bi=1B_i = 1Bi​=1 if the frequency of element iii in AAA is odd.
Bi=0B_i = 0Bi​=0 if the frequency of element iii in AAA is even.
Such an array BBB is called the parity encoding array of AAA.

For example, if A=[1,1,2,3]A = [1, 1, 2, 3]A=[1,1,2,3], then B=[0,1,1,0]B = [0, 1, 1, 0]B=[0,1,1,0].

Unfortunately, Chef completely forgot the array AAA and vaguely remembers the parity encoding array BBB. He is now wondering whether there exists any valid array AAA for which the parity encoding array is BBB. Can you help Chef?


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the size of the arrays AAA and BBB.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers B1,B2,…,BNB_1, B_2, \dots, B_NB1​,B2​,…,BN​ denoting the parity encoding array BBB.


Output Format
For each test case, output `YES` if there exists any valid array AAA for which the parity encoding array is BBB. Otherwise, output `NO`.


You may print each character of `YES` and `NO` in uppercase or lowercase (for example, `yes`, `yEs`, `Yes` will be considered identical).
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains integer N, the length of the arrays. Second line contains N space‑separated integers B1 … BN (each 0 or 1).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if there exists at least one array A that yields the given parity encoding array B, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: ones=2, N=4, N-ones=2 even → YES. Test case 2: ones=4, N=5, N-ones=1 odd → NO. Test case 3: ones=6, N=6, N-ones=0 even → YES.

solution.approach:
Let ci be the frequency of value i in the unknown array A. The condition B_i = 1 means ci must be odd, B_i = 0 means ci must be even. The smallest possible sum of all ci is the number of ones in B (each odd count can be 1, each even count can be 0). Any further increase must be by adding 2 to some ci, which keeps the parity unchanged. Therefore a feasible assignment exists iff:
1) number_of_ones ≤ N (we cannot exceed total length), and
2) (N - number_of_ones) is even (the remaining positions can be distributed in pairs). The algorithm simply counts the ones and checks the two conditions.
The construction itself is not required, only the existence test.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, total O(sum of N) ≤ 2·10^5.
